title: The Rate-Distortion-Polysemanticity Tradeoff in SAEs
text: arXiv 2026

title: The Rate-Distortion-Polysemanticity Tradeoff in SAEs
text:
Introduction
Sparse autoencoders have become the workhorse of mechanistic interpretability: trained to reconstruct a model’s activations through a sparse bottleneck, they promise to recover the underlying features a network uses (Cunningham et al. 2023). The hope is that these features are monosemantic — each one tracking a single human-legible concept rather than a blend of several (Bricken et al. 2023). In practice SAEs that reconstruct well with few active units tend to learn polysemantic features, and tightening one objective seems to slacken another.

Cunningham, Hoagy, Aidan Ewart, Logan Riggs, Robert Huben, and Lee Sharkey. 2023. “Sparse Autoencoders Find Highly Interpretable Features in Language Models.” arXiv Preprint arXiv:2309.08600.

Bricken, Trenton, Adly Templeton, Joshua Batson, et al. 2023. “Towards Monosemanticity: Decomposing Language Models with Dictionary Learning.” Transformer Circuits Thread.

Blau, Yochai, and Tomer Michaeli. 2019. “Rethinking Lossy Compression: The Rate-Distortion-Perception Tradeoff.” International Conference on Machine Learning.

Shannon, Claude E. 1959. “Coding Theorems for a Discrete Source with a Fidelity Criterion.” IRE National Convention Record 7: 142–63.
We argue this is not an accident of training but an information-theoretic constraint. Borrowing the rate-distortion-perception framing of lossy compression (Blau and Michaeli 2019; Shannon 1959), we add a third axis — polysemanticity — and show the three quantities trade off against each other. Superposition predicts that a model packs more concepts than it has dimensions, which is exactly what forces polysemantic codes.
A claim that deserves a caveat lives in a margin footnote.1
1 We assume access to ground-truth concepts when defining polysemanticity; the audit in §4 concerns proxies usable without them.

title: The Rate-Distortion-Polysemanticity Tradeoff in SAEs
text:
A rate-distortion-polysemanticity formulation
An SAE encodes an input x to a sparse code z_\theta(x) and decodes back to \hat{x}_\theta. Training minimizes reconstruction error under a sparsity penalty S with weight \lambda:

\arg\min_{\theta\in\Theta}\ \mathbb{E}\!\left[\,\lVert x - \hat{x}_\theta\rVert_2^2 + \lambda\, S\!\left(z_\theta(x)\right)\right].

We summarize a trained SAE by three scalars. The rate R(\theta) = \mathbb{E}\,\lVert z_\theta(x)\rVert_0 counts active features; the distortion D(\theta) = \mathbb{E}\,\lVert x-\hat{x}_\theta\rVert_2^2 is the reconstruction error; and the polysemanticity of a learned dictionary with concept-similarity matrix C\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times n} is

P(C) := \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\Bigl(1 - \max_{\ell} c_{i\ell}^2\Bigr)\ \in [0,1],

the average spread of each feature across ground-truth concepts. A feature aligned with a single concept contributes 0; one split evenly across concepts contributes near 1.


The RDP function. Collecting the three budgets, we study R^*(D_0,P_0) := \inf_\theta \{\, R(\theta) : D(\theta)\le D_0,\ P(\theta)\le P_0 \,\}, the smallest rate achievable under a joint distortion and polysemanticity budget. This is the SAE analogue of Shannon’s rate-distortion function with one extra constraint.
What does a dictionary actually look like at the two ends of the polysemanticity axis? The same handful of activations can be explained two ways. A polysemantic dictionary lets a few features each carry several concepts at once — when, say, cat, dog, fur, and paw tend to co-occur, two features can pack all four, keeping the rate low at the cost of a high P. A monosemantic dictionary insists that each feature track exactly one concept, driving P \to 0 but forcing four features on instead of two. The second option is more interpretable but simply needs more features active — which is precisely the rate the next section quantifies.

title: The Rate-Distortion-Polysemanticity Tradeoff in SAEs
section: Theory: tightening one budget pushes up the others
text:
Theory: tightening one budget pushes up the others
The central object is the RDP function R^*(D_0,P_0) defined in the margin. Two results characterize its shape.
Monotonicity. R^*(D_0,P_0) is nonincreasing in P_0: demanding more monosemantic features (a smaller P_0) can only raise the required rate at a fixed distortion. Tightening any one of the three budgets forces at least one of the other two up.
A rate tax for monosemanticity. Let an optimal polysemantic baseline reach distortion D_\infty with rate k. Any SAE that matches that distortion while being monosemantic must pay

R(\theta) \ \ge\ \mathbb{E}_{p_c}\lVert c\rVert_0 - \delta \ >\ k,

where \mathbb{E}_{p_c}\lVert c\rVert_0 is the expected number of co-occurring concepts and \delta a small slack. The premium is governed entirely by how concepts co-occur in the data: when concepts fire together, a monosemantic code must spend a separate feature on each, while a polysemantic code can reuse one.
Under an orthogonality assumption the loss collapses to a clean combinatorial form, \mathcal{L}(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{p_S}\!\left[\,|S| + |\hat S_\theta| - 2\,|S\cap\hat S_\theta|\,\right], the expected symmetric difference between true and reconstructed concept sets — making the rate tax explicit as the cost of resolving every co-occurrence.

The rate tax, made tangible
Read the bound as a dial on the polysemanticity budget P_0. As P_0 tightens from permissive toward strict monosemanticity, the optimal operating point must climb the rate axis to hold distortion fixed at D_0: it can no longer pack co-occurring concepts into a shared feature, so it spends a separate feature on each. The vertical gap that opens above the polysemantic optimum — the floor at rate k — is the rate tax, and the dual statement holds on the distortion axis. Both taxes share the same driver: the conservation law D + R = \mathbb{E}_{p_c}\lVert c\rVert_0 on the monosemantic class, which is why the price is set entirely by how often concepts co-occur in the data.

title: The Rate-Distortion-Polysemanticity Tradeoff in SAEs
text:
Results
The predicted frontier is a staircase: at a fixed polysemanticity budget the optimal rate steps up as the distortion budget tightens, and the whole monosemantic curve sits above the polysemantic one by the rate tax. The paper measures exactly this on synthetic data (Figure 1).








Figure 1: Empirical RDP frontier for TopK SAEs on a synthetic DGP (n=20 concepts in \mathbb{R}^6, \sim\!0.6 features active per sample; sweep over rate k\in\{1,\dots,8\} and monosemanticity penalty \lambda\in\{0,\dots,100\}, 7 seeds). (a) the full (P,D,R) surface; (b) P vs D at rate bounds k\le k_0; (c) P vs R at distortion budgets D\le D_0; (d) the R vs D frontier at polysemanticity budgets P\le P_0 — the predicted staircase, shifting right (worse rate and distortion) as monosemanticity is demanded.



From the original paper (Mencattini, Montagna & Locatello, 2026).


On synthetic data — n=20 orthogonal concepts in \mathbb{R}^6 with roughly 0.6 features active per sample — sweeping the code size k\in\{1,\dots,8\} and the monosemanticity penalty \lambda\in\{0,\dots,100\} reproduces the predicted staircase — the rightmost panel of Figure 1. The more interesting test is whether existing interpretability metrics move in the RDP-predicted direction. Auditing nine polysemanticity proxies on Gemma TopK/ReLU SAEs from SAEBench (Table 1), most do not: the automated-interpretability and unlearning scores are anti-correlated with the theory, while feature-splitting and sparse-probing only weakly agree.



Table 1: Audit of polysemanticity proxies on Gemma TopK SAEs. Lower V is better; higher \rho is better; a random baseline gives V=0.5.











Metric
Violation V(\hat P) ↓
Rank corr. \rho(\hat P) ↑
Verdict




AutoInterp
0.886
−0.68
Anti-correlated


Unlearning
0.762
−0.54
Anti-correlated

0.441
+0.21
Weakly agrees


Sparse Probing
0.351
+0.19
Weakly agrees






The full audit across all nine proxies — on both TopK and ReLU SAEs — tells the same story (Figure 2): violation rates cluster at or above the random baseline, and most rank correlations are negative.








Figure 2: V (left two panels, lower is better; dashed line marks the random baseline V=\tfrac12) and \rho (right two panels, higher is better) for the nine SAEBench proxies on TopK and ReLU Gemma SAEs. AutoInterp and Unlearning sit furthest in the wrong direction; only Splitting and Sparse Probing weakly agree.



From the original paper (Mencattini, Montagna & Locatello, 2026).


No widely adopted metric clearly tracks the RDP-predicted direction, and the strongest-looking proxies point the wrong way — a caution for studies that read monosemanticity off these scores.

title: The Rate-Distortion-Polysemanticity Tradeoff in SAEs
text:
Conclusion
Polysemanticity in SAEs is, at bottom, a property of the data: when concepts co-occur, monosemantic features cost extra rate and extra distortion, and no amount of tuning removes that tax (Elhage et al. 2022). The practical implication is that better interpretability will come from architectural and optimization choices that account for co-occurrence structure, not from chasing monosemanticity as a free objective. Full references appear in the margin; a copyable BibTeX entry for this paper is generated automatically.



Elhage, Nelson, Tristan Hume, Catherine Olsson, et al. 2022. “Toy Models of Superposition.” Transformer Circuits Thread.

title: CLAI Research Group
text:
Locatello Group · ISTA



Today’s models describe the world as they found it. We want AI that grasps why — learning causal representations and models from data, so it can reason about interventions, domain shifts, and the structure beneath what it sees.

Browse papers Meet the team →
We learn causal structure from data, so that AI can reason about interventions and distribution shifts rather than only fit the conditions it was trained on. Our work spans:

title: About
text:
The Locatello Group, led by Assistant Professor Francesco Locatello at the Institute of Science and Technology Austria (ISTA), works on causal learning and artificial intelligence.
Modern machine learning is still limited to a superficial description of reality that only holds when the experimental conditions are fixed. It largely ignores interventions in the world, domain shifts, and temporal structure. Our group focuses on learning causal representations and causal models from data to address these issues. Through rigorous theory and scalable algorithms, we enable AI agents to understand cause-and-effect relationships, the effect of interventions, and distribution changes underlying the data they encounter.
Advances in causal learning have promising applications in machine learning and artificial intelligence, including robustness, explainability, and fairness in recognition, reasoning, and planning tasks. Most importantly, their impact extends to discovering new scientific knowledge from massive amounts of data and serving as the interface between people and complex systems for decision-making.
Causal learning emerges by discovering structural knowledge about the data-generating process and meaningful abstractions that allow for well-defined causal relations. Research questions span theoretical identifiability and methodological innovations in causal learning, discovering objects and abstract entities, their factors of variations, and their causal relations. We apply these methods to solve open problems in machine learning and enable new applications in the sciences.

Contact

Institute group page: ista.ac.at/en/research/locatello-group

The Rate-Distortion-Polysemanticity Tradeoff in SAEs



Interpretability

Theory



Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) that accurately reconstruct their input (low distortion) while using few active features (low rate) often fail to learn monosemantic, human-interpretable features. We cast this tension as a rate-distortion-polysemanticity (RDP) tradeoff and show, theoretically and empirically, that enforcing monosemanticity raises both the rate and the distortion an SAE must pay. The optimal degree of polysemanticity is set by the data distribution — specifically by how ground-truth concepts co-occur — rather than by optimization choices. We derive necessary conditions that any polysemanticity measure must satisfy in realistic settings and audit nine proxies used on LLM-trained SAEs, finding that most fail to track the RDP-predicted direction. Polysemanticity is therefore a data-level constraint, not merely a training artifact.





May 14, 2026


Tommaso Mencattini, Francesco Montagna, Francesco Locatello


arXiv


2026
